abstract:
This paper investigates the challenge of channel estimation in multi-cell interference-limited networks, specifically focusing on the "pilot contamination" effect in both finite and large-scale MIMO systems. Traditional estimation methods struggle to distinguish between desired signals and interference when pilot sequences are reused across cells, creating a performance bottleneck that does not vanish even with an infinite number of antennas.

We propose a novel, coordinated estimation framework that leverages the second-order statistical information (covariance) of user channels. By exploiting the spatial structure of the channel—specifically the finite angle-of-arrival (AOA) spread—we demonstrate that base stations can effectively discriminate between desired and interfering users even when their pilot sequences are identical. Analytically, we prove that if the signal subspaces of the desired and interfering channel covariances are non-overlapping, the pilot contamination effect vanishes completely as the number of antennas grows. 

To operationalize these findings, we introduce a low-rate coordination protocol using a covariance-aware pilot assignment strategy. This approach selectively groups users to maximize the spatial orthogonality of their channel statistics. Numerical simulations confirm that our method provides significant performance gains in terms of both estimation accuracy and achievable downlink rates, outperforming conventional frameworks even with moderate antenna array sizes and in scenarios with imperfect spatial separation.
